english: pa (pa) wandering (contagious) disease, = yams (yams) Sch. (yáṅ-pa (yang pa) to run about, to wander Sch., is rather doubtful).

1. member, limb, yáṅ-lag lṅa (yan lag lnga) arms, legs, and head Mñg.; yáṅ-lag skyóṅ-can (yan lag skyon can) an injured or defective limb Lex.; yáṅ-lag nyams-pa (yan lag nyams pa) weak in the limbs, decrepit, crazy, = żá-ba (zha ba) Lex. -- 2. fig. branch of a river, branch of a tree; dge-bsnyén-gyi yáṅ-lag gzúṅ-bar bgyío (dge bsnyen gyi yan lag gzung bar bgyio) Do. was explained: I wish to be counted a branch, i.e. a member, of the community of novices; appendage, something subordinate to a greater thing, like branch-establishment Tar. 175, 3; also with reference to books: appendix, supplement Tar. -- 3. branch, section, separate part of a doctrine or science, frq., a particular head, point, thought, in a treatise.

resp. for p'a (pha), father, rgyál-po yab yum gsum (rgyal po yab yum gsum) Glr. the king and his two consorts; rgyál-po yab yum (rgyal po yab yum) denotes also king and queen as father and mother to the country Glr.; yab rgyál-po-la gsól-to (yab rgyal po la gsol to) Dzl. he said to his royal father; yab-srás (yab sras) father and son, in a spiritual sense: master and disciple; yab-més (yab mes) 1. father and grandfather. 2. progenitor, ancestors Glr.

1. to lock, lock up, secure, cover over Sch., yab-ča (yab cha) things well secured, under safe keeping; yáb-yob-pa (yab yob pa) to hide, conceal Sch.; gyab (g.yab) or gyab-sa (g.yab sa) covered place, covert, shelter Sch.; yab riṅ-po (yab ring po) portico, veranda, e.g. of the monastery at Tashilhunpo; yab-ras (yab ras) awning, tent Sch. -- 2. C. to skim, to scoop off, from the surface of a fluid. -- 3. W. to move to and fro, hither and thither, v. gyób-pa (g.yob pa). --

1. the act of fanning, waving, lág-pa yáb-mo byéd-ciṅ c'í-ba (lag pa yab mo byed cing chi ba) dying whilst waving the hand to and fro, considered as a sign of peace Do.; gós-kyi yáb-mo byéd-pa (gos kyi yab mo byed pa) Glr. to beckon by waving with one's clothes; hence fig. -- 2. the bringing on, provoking, dgrá-boi (dgra boi) of an enemy Mil., p'uṅ-gzíi (phung gzii) a calamity Mil.; yáb-mo ḥdè'-pa (yab mo 'de' pa) or gyáb-pa (g.yab pa) to beckon to come, to bring (something adverse) upon one's self. -- 3. fan, rṅa-yáb (rnga yab) a. a yak-tail fan Cs. b. kettle-drum stick Sch.; sbraṅ-yáb (sbrang yab) fly-brush Cs.; rluṅ-yáb (rlung yab) ventilating- or cooling-fan Cs.; bsil-yáb (bsil yab) pankah (Hind.), a large fan suspended from the ceiling and set in motion by means of a string. -- 4. sail Cs.?

1. Sch.: coarsely, roughly, of a coarse make, rough-hewn. -- 2. Mil.: c'ui (chui) ká-na (ka na) pár-la (phar la) yám-me (yam me) yśegs (gshegs) he walked softly gliding across the water to the other bank.
Cs. also yam-yúm (yam yum) Thgr. tottering, not steady Cs. yam-yóm byéd-pa (yam yom byed pa) to totter.
yams-nád (yams nad) Cs., nad-yáms (nad yams) Glr. epidemic or contagious disease, plague, má-yams (ma yams) a plague caused by evil spirits, v. má-mo (ma mo).

from ya (ya), up, upward, also yár-la (yar la), e.g. gzigs-pa (gzigs pa) to look up Glr., yár-ₒgro (yar 'gro) már-ₒgro (mar 'gro) byéd-pa (byed pa) Glr. to travel up and down; yar mar ₒcág-pa (yar mar 'chag pa) B., *kyód-če* (kyod ce) W. resp., to walk up and down; yár-la kyer śog (yar la kyer shog) bring or fetch up Pth.; yar ma sgyugs (sgyugs) mar ma ₒtón-par (mar ma 'thon par) Pth. as it would go off neither upward by vomiting, nor downward; yár-nas már-la (yar nas mar la) from top to bottom; yar ₒtón-pa (yar 'thon pa) Thgy. to come up again, from a depth; *yar mar tsáṅ-ma-ru* (yar mar tsang ma ru) W. in every direction, all over; in such expressions as yar laṅ-ba (yar lang ba) to rise, get up, yar ₒpél-ba (yar 'phel ba) to increase, it stands pleon.; yár-la (yar la) also denotes a relation to that which is higher, the intercourse with, the deportment towards, superiors (már-la (mar la) the contrary) Glr.; esp. with reference to the transmigration of souls and their final deliverance: yar gcód-pa (yar gcod pa) to cut off the way to the three upper classes of beings, the so-called 'good natures', yar skyé-ba (yar skye ba) to be reborn in the upper classes, the reverse of which is mar ₒgríb-pa (mar 'grib pa) to sink down to the lower; yar ₒdrén-pa (yar 'dren pa) to draw or lift up to heaven.
